title: First Ever President's Rule - Punjab 1951
description: First invocation of Article 356 in Indian history. Governor Chandulal Trivedi recommended President's Rule following political instability and inability to form a stable government after the first general elections. This set the precedent for future misuse of this constitutional provision.

sources.title: President's Rule in States - Historical Data

notes: Historical - needs primary source verification

title: Kerala Communist Government Dismissal 1959
description: Governor Ramakrishna Rao recommended dismissal of E.M.S. Namboodiripad's Communist government under Article 356 during the 'Vimochana Samaram' (Liberation Struggle). This controversial dismissal of India's first democratically elected Communist government remains a landmark case of gubernatorial overreach. The Congress-led central government used this to remove a political opponent despite the government having majority.

sources.title: 1959 Kerala Liberation Struggle
Article 356 - The Emergency Provision

notes: First dismissal of Communist government; Vimochana Samaram (Liberation Struggle)

title: Emergency Dismissal of DMK Government 1976
description: During the Emergency, Governor K.K. Shah recommended dismissal of the DMK government led by M. Karunanidhi under Article 356. The Indira Gandhi government used allegations of corruption and anti-national activities as justification. This remains one of the most controversial uses of President's Rule during the Emergency period.

sources.title: President's Rule in States - Historical Data

notes: Emergency era - extensively documented

title: Post-Emergency Mass State Dismissals 1977
description: After the Janata Party won the 1977 elections, governors recommended dismissal of Congress governments in 9 states simultaneously - Uttar Pradesh, Madhya Pradesh, Rajasthan, Punjab, Haryana, Bihar, Orissa, Himachal Pradesh, and West Bengal. This revenge dissolution mirrored what Congress had done to opposition states previously.

sources.title: President's Rule in States - Historical Data

notes: Mass dismissal - multiple states simultaneously

title: Indira Gandhi Returns - 9 States Dismissed 1980
description: When Indira Gandhi returned to power in 1980, she orchestrated the dismissal of 9 non-Congress state governments through governors - mirroring what Janata had done in 1977. States affected included Tamil Nadu, Madhya Pradesh, Rajasthan, Punjab, Bihar, Uttar Pradesh, Gujarat, Maharashtra, and Orissa. This established the pattern of using governors as political tools.

sources.title: President's Rule in States - Historical Data

notes: Pattern of misuse - extensively documented

title: Jagmohan Dismisses G.M. Shah Government 1986
description: Governor Jagmohan controversially dismissed the G.M. Shah government in Jammu & Kashmir. This action was seen as politically motivated, with Jagmohan later returning as Governor during the 1990 militancy outbreak. His role in both dismissals became highly controversial.

sources.title: President's Rule in States - Historical Data

notes: Jagmohan's controversial tenure

title: Punjab - Barnala Government Dismissal 1987
description: Governor S.S. Ray recommended dismissal of Surjit Singh Barnala's Akali Dal government amid the Punjab insurgency. This began the longest period of President's Rule in Punjab's history, lasting nearly 5 years during the peak of militancy. The state saw massive human rights violations during this period.

sources.title: President's Rule in States - Historical Data

notes: Punjab militancy era - extensive documentation

title: S.R. Bommai Dismissal - Landmark Case Origin 1989
description: The dismissal of S.R. Bommai's Janata Dal government in Karnataka by the Governor led to the landmark S.R. Bommai v. Union of India case (decided 1994). The Supreme Court later ruled this dismissal unconstitutional and established that Article 356 is judicially reviewable. This case fundamentally changed how President's Rule could be imposed.

sources.title: S.R. Bommai v. Union of India (1994)

notes: Landmark case - full judgment available on Indian Kanoon
sc_precedents: S.R. Bommai v Union of India (1994)

title: Kashmir Militancy - Governor's Rule 1990
description: Governor Jagmohan was brought back to J&K as militancy erupted. He immediately recommended dismissal of the Farooq Abdullah government, beginning over 6 years of President's Rule during the peak insurgency period. Jagmohan's actions, including the exodus of Kashmiri Pandits, remain deeply controversial.

sources.title: President's Rule in States - Historical Data

notes: Kashmir insurgency beginning

title: Post-Babri Demolition - 4 BJP States Dismissed 1992
description: Following the Babri Masjid demolition, governors in Uttar Pradesh, Madhya Pradesh, Himachal Pradesh, and Rajasthan recommended President's Rule. All four BJP-ruled states were dismissed within days. While politically motivated, this action was later upheld by the Supreme Court as constitutional given the extraordinary circumstances.

sources.title: S.R. Bommai v. Union of India (1994) - Post-Babri Context

notes: Bommai judgment was partly in response to post-Babri dismissals
sc_precedents: S.R. Bommai v Union of India (1994)

title: Bihar - Rabri Devi Dismissal Reversed 1999
description: Governor Sunder Singh Bhandari recommended dismissal of Rabri Devi's RJD government citing breakdown of law and order. However, the President returned the recommendation, and Rabri Devi was restored. This case showed post-Bommai constraints on arbitrary dismissals.

sources.title: Rameshwar Prasad v. Union of India - Bihar Case

notes: Rabri Devi dismissal reversed; floor test principles established
sc_precedents: Rameshwar Prasad v Union of India (2006)

title: Karnataka Coalition Collapse - Governor's Role 2007
description: Governor Rameshwar Thakur's handling of the JD(S)-BJP coalition collapse led to President's Rule. When BJP withdrew support, the Governor's sequence of inviting parties and timing of floor tests was criticized. This case highlighted continuing controversies around gubernatorial discretion.

sources.title: B.S. Yeddyurappa v. State of Karnataka (2007)

notes: JD(S)-BJP coalition collapse; Governor role in government formation

title: Arunachal Pradesh - Governor Prepones Session 2016
description: Governor J.P. Rajkhowa preponed the Assembly session without cabinet advice, enabling a rebel faction to remove the Speaker. The Supreme Court in Nabam Rebia case declared this unconstitutional, establishing that governors cannot act against the Assembly's constitutional functioning. President's Rule was subsequently imposed but later struck down.

sources.title: Nabam Rebia v Deputy Speaker - Reconsideration

notes: Nabam Rebia landmark case

title: Uttarakhand President's Rule Struck Down 2016
description: Governor K.K. Paul recommended President's Rule during Congress infighting. The Nainital High Court struck it down as unconstitutional, finding the Governor's report was based on incorrect facts. The Harish Rawat government was restored. This reinforced post-Bommai judicial review.

sources.title: Union of India v. Harish Rawat - Uttarakhand President Rule Case

notes: HC struck down President Rule; SC upheld
sc_precedents: Union of India v Harish Rawat (2016)

title: Karnataka - Governor Invites BJP Despite Fewer Seats 2018
description: Governor Vajubhai Vala invited BJP's B.S. Yediyurappa to form government despite BJP winning 104 seats versus Congress-JD(S) combined 117. He gave 15 days for floor test. Supreme Court intervened, ordered floor test in 24 hours. Yediyurappa resigned before the test. Governor's conduct was widely criticized.

sources.title: H.D. Kumaraswamy v. Union of India - Karnataka Floor Test Order

notes: SC ordered floor test within 24 hours; Yeddyurappa resigned before test
sc_precedents: H.D. Kumaraswamy v Union of India (2018)

title: J&K Assembly Dissolved - Article 370 Preparation 2018
description: Governor Satya Pal Malik dissolved the J&K Assembly when multiple parties were attempting to form government, citing horse-trading. This prevented any elected government from challenging the subsequent Article 370 abrogation. The timing raised serious questions about gubernatorial complicity in the constitutional changes.

sources.title: Abrogation of Article 370 - J&K Case

notes: Assembly dissolution preceded Article 370 abrogation

title: West Bengal Governor-CM Continuous Conflict 2019-2022
description: Governor Jagdeep Dhankhar's tenure was marked by constant conflict with CM Mamata Banerjee. Issues included delayed assent to bills, public criticism of the state government, university appointment disputes, and allegations of surveillance. The Governor's social media presence and public comments were unprecedented.

sources.title: Maharashtra MLAs Case - Governor Powers Referenced

notes: Ongoing conflict; Governor Dhankhar now Vice President

title: Maharashtra 5 AM Swearing - SC Strikes Down 2019
description: Governor B.S. Koshyari recommended President's Rule, then revoked it at 5:47 AM to swear in Devendra Fadnavis as CM with NCP's Ajit Pawar as Deputy CM. This happened secretly before dawn. Supreme Court took suo motu cognizance, ordered floor test. The government fell within 3 days. One of the most dramatic gubernatorial controversies.

sources.title: Maharashtra case: SC issues notices to Centre, State govt, CM Fadnavis, Dy CM Ajit Pawar
Maharashtra MLAs Disqualification - References 2019 Crisis

notes: Dramatic - extensive video/documentation
sc_precedents: Shiv Sena v Union of India (2019)

title: Rajasthan Assembly Session Delay 2020
description: During the Sachin Pilot rebellion, Governor Kalraj Mishra delayed calling an Assembly session despite CM Ashok Gehlot's recommendation. He imposed conditions including COVID protocols and 21-day notice. After political pressure and legal threats, he eventually called the session. The delay aided the rebel faction.

sources.title: Rajasthan Assembly Session - Governor Delay Case 2020

notes: Governor eventually called session after political pressure

title: Governor vs CM: Public Feud Over Post-Poll Violence
description: Governor Jagdeep Dhankhar engaged in an unprecedented public confrontation with CM Mamata Banerjee over post-election violence in 2021. He made multiple unscheduled visits to violence-affected areas, held press conferences criticizing the state government, tweeted accusations against the CM, and summoned officials directly - all actions the state termed as exceeding his constitutional mandate. The standoff continued until his elevation to Vice President.
raj_bhavan_response: Constitutional duty to protect citizens; criticized 'jungle raj'
legislative_pushback: CM accused Governor of being BJP spokesperson; called for his removal

title: Kerala University Laws Amendment Bills Withheld
description: Governor Arif Mohammad Khan has been sitting on seven university amendment bills passed by the Kerala Assembly since 2021. These bills seek to remove the Governor as Chancellor of state universities and transfer appointment powers to the state government. The Governor claims the bills are unconstitutional while the state argues they are within legislative competence. The Supreme Court has intervened, asking the Governor to take a decision.
sc_precedents: State of Tamil Nadu v Governor (2025)

sources.title: State of Kerala v Governor for State of Kerala
Kerala Governor Bills - Day 1 Hearing

raj_bhavan_response: Bills under examination; constitutional concerns raised
legislative_pushback: Kerala Assembly passed resolutions condemning delay; CM approached Supreme Court

title: NEET Exemption Bill Withheld
description: The Tamil Nadu Assembly passed a bill exempting the state from NEET (National Eligibility cum Entrance Test) for medical admissions, arguing that the centralized exam disadvantages rural and Tamil-medium students. Governor R.N. Ravi has refused to give assent or return the bill, effectively pocket-vetoing it. The state cites its educational autonomy while the Governor's inaction prevents any judicial review.
bill_name: Tamil Nadu Admission to MBBS Course (Prohibition of NEET) Bill 2022
sc_precedents: State of Tamil Nadu v Governor (2025)

sources.title: Pendency of Bills Before Tamil Nadu Governor - Judgment Summary
SC Effectuates 10 Pending TN Bills

legislative_pushback: CM Stalin approached Supreme Court seeking direction to Governor

title: Maharashtra Government Collapse: Midnight Swearing-In
description: Governor Bhagat Singh Koshyari swore in Eknath Shinde as CM at midnight, immediately after accepting Uddhav Thackeray's resignation, without waiting for a floor test. Critics alleged the Governor acted in haste to benefit the BJP-backed faction. The Supreme Court later upheld the government but criticized the manner of formation, noting procedural irregularities in how the Governor handled the crisis.
raj_bhavan_response: Acted within constitutional provisions; government has majority
legislative_pushback: Uddhav Thackeray faction filed SC petition challenging formation

title: Governor Threatens to 'Sack' Ministers
description: Governor Arif Mohammad Khan publicly threatened to 'dismiss' state ministers and called a minister a 'criminal' during a press conference, actions that have no constitutional basis. The Governor cannot dismiss ministers under Article 164 - only the CM advises on ministerial appointments. The confrontation was triggered by ministers criticizing the Governor's handling of university bills.

title: Punjab Bills Withheld - SC Reprimand 2022-2023
description: Governor Banwarilal Purohit withheld assent to multiple bills passed by the AAP government including on police and universities. The Supreme Court in State of Punjab v. Principal Secretary to Governor case reprimanded the Governor for effectively exercising a pocket veto, establishing that governors cannot indefinitely sit on bills.

sources.title: Presidential Reference on Governor Powers - Includes Punjab

notes: Punjab case part of broader Presidential Reference on Governor powers

title: Punjab Farm Bills Delayed
description: Governor Banwarilal Purohit delayed action on bills passed by Punjab Assembly related to agricultural reforms and MSP guarantees. The state government accused the Governor of deliberately stalling legislation that would benefit farmers, while the Raj Bhavan cited need for legal examination.

title: Telangana Education Bill Stalled
description: Governor Tamilisai Soundararajan delayed action on education-related bills passed by the Telangana Assembly, including amendments to university governance. The then-BRS government accused her of acting as a 'BJP agent' to obstruct state legislation.

title: West Bengal Property Tax Amendment Pending
description: Governor C.V. Ananda Bose has held up the West Bengal Property Tax Amendment Bill without giving assent or returning it. The bill sought to rationalize property tax collection in urban areas. The state government views this as another instance of the Governor obstructing routine legislation.

title: Vice-Chancellor Appointment Dispute
description: Governor R.N. Ravi, as Chancellor of Tamil Nadu universities, attempted to appoint Vice-Chancellors without following state government recommendations. He rejected the state's panel of candidates and sought to impose his own choices, leading to a standoff. The Supreme Court has since ruled that Chancellors cannot indefinitely sit on VC appointments.

title: Lok Ayukta Amendment Bill Returned
description: Governor Arif Mohammad Khan returned the Kerala Lok Ayukta (Amendment) Bill to the Assembly, objecting to provisions that would dilute the anti-corruption body's powers. The state government resubmitted the bill unchanged, and the Governor eventually gave assent under pressure, but the delay sparked another round of Governor-Government friction.

title: Governor-CM Confrontation at Raj Bhavan
description: Governor C.V. Ananda Bose summoned Chief Minister Mamata Banerjee to Raj Bhavan and allegedly attempted to publicly lecture her on governance. The CM walked out after objecting to the Governor's tone and conduct. The Governor later made public statements criticizing the state government's law and order situation, which the CM termed as 'interference in state affairs'.
raj_bhavan_response: Governor expressed concern over law and order; denied improper conduct
legislative_pushback: CM publicly accused Governor of overstepping constitutional role

title: Assembly Address Walkout Over 'Dravidian Model' Reference
description: Governor R.N. Ravi walked out of the Tamil Nadu Assembly after refusing to read portions of his address that referenced 'Dravidian Model of Development' and social justice pioneer Periyar. He unilaterally declared the speech 'delivered' and left, while the Assembly Speaker ruled the address was not delivered. The Governor later released his own version omitting references to Periyar, Ambedkar, and social justice policies.
raj_bhavan_response: Governor's office claimed address was delivered; released modified version
legislative_pushback: Assembly Speaker ruled address not delivered; DMK condemned as constitutional violation
